title: Causal last-k-lap sequence embedding as features for the booster
summary: Recent F1 pit-stop work reports strongest gains from sequence models, and dynamic survival literature shows longitudinal prefixes matter more than single-row snapshots.
rationale: Recent F1 pit-stop work reports strongest gains from sequence models, and dynamic survival literature shows longitudinal prefixes matter more than single-row snapshots. Your current best runs are almost entirely static row features, so a strict history-only encoder may add signal without replacing the tabular stack.
implementation_hint: Within each Driver-Year-Race sequence, build history-only windows over the last 3-8 laps using LapTime (s), LapTime_Delta, TyreLife, Cumulative_Degradation, Position, Position_Change, Compound, Stint, and PitStop. Train a tiny GRU/TCN only on train folds, export its penultimate-layer embedding or next-lap logit as 1-3 numeric features, and feed those into the existing GBDT pipeline.
expected_effect: Should improve cases where identical current-row states have different short-term pit intent because recent pace loss or traffic changed abruptly; likely modest but real CV lift if leakage is controlled.
risk: Easy leakage trap if windows cross fold boundaries or include post-row information; end-to-end deep models may underperform if used alone, so keep it as feature generation or a blend component.
